content.problemStatement:
Chef is a wanted criminal, and NNN police officers are up for catching him. The officers want to catch Chef no matter the cost, and Chef also wants to eliminate as many officers as possible (preferably everyone) before getting caught (or before running away after eliminating everyone). Neither the officers nor Chef will run away before accomplishing their goals.

Chef and the officers are on a one-dimensional grid with coordinates ranging from −1010-10^{10}−1010 to 101010^{10}1010. Chef is initially standing at coordinate CCC, and the iii-th officer is initially at coordinate PiP_iPi​. The officers and Chef then take turns to move, with the officer team moving first.

During their turn, officers will have to move to an adjacent unoccupied cell one by one, in any order they want. Every officer will have to move. At every moment of time, no two officers can be in the same cell, and also no officer can be in the same cell as Chef. If the officer is unable to move to an adjacent unoccupied cell, he is eliminated (and the cell he was in becomes unoccupied). **Note that the officer team will try to move to eliminate as few officers as possible.** For example, with P=[2,3,4]P = [2, 3, 4]P=[2,3,4] and C=6C = 6C=6, then the next positions of the officers can be [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3], [1,2,5][1, 2, 5][1,2,5], [1,4,5][1, 4, 5][1,4,5], or [3,4,5][3, 4, 5][3,4,5]. However, with P=[2,3,4]P = [2, 3, 4]P=[2,3,4] and C=5C = 5C=5, the only possible set of the next positions of the officers is [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3].

After the officer team's turn, Chef also moves to an adjacent unoccupied cell, or gets caught if he cannot find any adjacent unoccupied cell. The process then repeats until either Chef is caught, or all officers are eliminated and Chef runs away.

You need to find out the maximum number of officers that can be eliminated by Chef, and if Chef can run away or not.

As a reminder, two cells with coordinates XXX and YYY are adjacent if and only if ∣X−Y∣=1|X - Y| = 1∣X−Y∣=1.


Input Format

The first line contains TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN - the number of officers.
The second line of each test case contains a single integer CCC - the initial coordinate of Chef.
The third line of each test case contains NNN integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \dots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ - the initial coordinates of the officers.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a single line two space-separated integers: the first integer is the maximum number of officers that can be eliminated by Chef, and the second integer is 111 if Chef runs away or −1-1−1 if Chef will be caught.
constraints: All coordinates are distinct and Pi ≠ C
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer N, the number of officers.
- The second line contains an integer C, Chef's initial coordinate.
- The third line contains N distinct integers P1 P2 … PN, the initial coordinates of the officers.
All coordinates are within [-10^10, 10^10] and are pairwise different from each other and from C.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with two space‑separated integers: the maximum number of officers that can be eliminated by Chef, and 1 if Chef can run away (all officers eliminated) or -1 if Chef will eventually be caught.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Chef chooses to always move to the left (i.e. to the smaller coordinate); this forces the officer at coordinate 111 to always move to the left, and eventually being cornered and eliminated. However, the officer at coordinate 444 cannot be eliminated, and hence Chef will be caught at the end.
Test case 222: Similarly, Chef chooses to always move to the left, and eventually eliminating the only officer, thus running away at the end.

solution.approach:
Observe that the relative order of Chef and the officers never changes – nobody can jump over another person. Therefore the line can be split into a left side (positions < C) and a right side (positions > C). During a turn each officer must move one step left or right to an empty cell. To minimise eliminations the officers will always keep a free cell on at least one side unless they are forced into a dead‑end.

An officer is eliminated only when both adjacent cells are occupied at the moment of his move. This can happen only for the officer that is immediately next to Chef and also has another officer directly on his other side, i.e. when three consecutive cells are occupied like …, x‑1, x, x+1 with Chef at x+1. Consequently Chef can eliminate a *contiguous block* of officers that are placed on the same side of him in consecutive cells.

If we look at the cells C‑1, C‑2, C‑3, … and count how many of them are occupied by officers, we obtain the number of officers that Chef can eliminate by always moving left. The same holds for the right side with cells C+1, C+2, … .

Let:
- leftCnt = number of i ≥ 1 such that (C‑i) is present among the officers.
- rightCnt = number of i ≥ 1 such that (C+i) is present among the officers.
The answer for the maximum eliminations is max(leftCnt, rightCnt).

Chef can run away only when *all* officers lie on the same side of him **and** they occupy exactly the consecutive cells counted above, i.e. leftCnt == N (all on the left) or rightCnt == N (all on the right). In any other configuration at least one officer remains on the opposite side and will eventually catch Chef.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Insert all officer positions into an unordered_set for O(1) look‑ups.
2. Compute leftCnt by checking C‑1, C‑2, … until a cell is missing.
3. Compute rightCnt similarly with C+1, C+2, … .
4. Determine if all officers are on the left (every Pi < C) or all on the right (every Pi > C).
5. maxEliminated = max(leftCnt, rightCnt).
6. runAway = 1 if (allLeft && leftCnt == N) or (allRight && rightCnt == N), otherwise -1.
7. Output maxEliminated and runAway.

The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case (building the set and a linear scan of at most N cells) and uses O(N) additional memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of officers.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the hash set storing officer positions.
